subject: ap-psychology
title: Biological Bases of Behavior and Social Psychology — Concept Application
stimulus: Ruth is bored at home alone, so she decides to watch a scary movie. At the most tense part of the movie, when a large, scary-looking creature is being chased through the village by an angry mob, Ruth's roommate Lynn comes home unexpectedly and flips on the lights. Ruth is startled and spills her hot chocolate on herself.

parts.prompt: Explain how Optimal arousal theory relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that Ruth is motivated to watch the scary movie in order to increase her level of arousal (she is bored — below her optimal arousal level). OR the response may indicate that after being startled, Ruth does something to lower her arousal back to a desired level.

required_elements: Ruth is bored (below desired arousal), so she watches the scary movie to raise her arousal to an optimal level.
alternatives.correct_example: Ruth is at a lower arousal than she would like (hence the boredom). So, she decides to watch the scary movie to bring up her arousal level to a more desirable level for her.

required_elements: After being startled by Lynn, Ruth does something to lower her now-elevated arousal back to a desired level.
alternatives.correct_example: After being startled by Lynn, Ruth turns on calming music to reduce her arousal back to her optimal level.

wrong_examples: The optimal arousal theory says that people perform best when their arousal level is neither too high nor too low.
Ruth's arousal increases when she is startled by her roommate.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Optimal arousal theory holds that organisms are motivated to seek a preferred level of stimulation — when below optimal (bored), they seek stimulation; when above optimal, they seek to reduce it. The response must describe Ruth moving toward her optimal level. An acceptable response explains that Ruth is bored (below optimal arousal) and watches the scary movie to raise her arousal, OR that after being startled she takes action to lower it. Simply citing the Yerkes-Dodson law without describing movement toward an optimal level does not earn the point. Simply noting that Ruth's arousal increases does not describe motivation toward optimal arousal.

sample_responses.response: Ruth is bored (below her desired arousal level), so she watches a scary movie to bring her arousal up to a more stimulating, optimal level.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies that boredom = below optimal arousal and that the scary movie is chosen to raise arousal toward the optimal level.

sample_responses.response: After Lynn startles her and her arousal spikes too high, Ruth takes several deep breaths to calm down and bring her arousal back to a comfortable level.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable alternate direction — after being over-aroused (startled), Ruth acts to reduce arousal back toward optimal.

sample_responses.response: The optimal arousal theory says that people perform best when their arousal level is neither too high nor too low.
sample_responses.commentary: Restates the Yerkes-Dodson law as a general principle without describing how Ruth's behavior in the scenario is motivated by seeking an optimal arousal level.

sample_responses.response: Ruth's arousal increases when she is startled by her roommate.
sample_responses.commentary: Simply noting an arousal change does not demonstrate motivation toward an optimal level — there is no behavioral response toward correcting the arousal level.

parts.prompt: Explain how Motor neurons relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that motor neurons are enabling a specific physical movement in the scenario. Acceptable actions: Ruth reaching for a towel, the creature running, Ruth jumping when startled. Responses that describe motor neurons performing multiple different functions simultaneously do not earn the point.

required_elements: Motor neurons enable Ruth to reach for a towel to clean up the spilled hot chocolate.
alternatives.correct_example: Motor neurons allow Ruth to reach for a towel to clean up the spilled hot chocolate.

required_elements: The creature's motor neurons allow him to run through the village.
alternatives.correct_example: The creature's motor neurons allow him to run through the village.

required_elements: Ruth reflexively jumps when Lynn startles her.
alternatives.correct_example: Ruth reflexively jumps when Lynn startles her.

wrong_examples: Ruth's motor neurons allow Lynn to flip on the lights and allow Ruth to watch the movie, taste the hot chocolate, and feel burned when she spills the hot chocolate on herself.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Motor neurons carry signals from the central nervous system to muscles, enabling a specific physical movement. The response must identify one specific physical movement in the scenario and attribute it to motor neurons. Acceptable examples include Ruth reaching for a towel, the creature running through the village, or Ruth reflexively jumping when startled. Responses that include multiple different functions (e.g., attributing watching, tasting, and feeling to motor neurons) do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Motor neurons allow Ruth to reach for a towel to clean up the spilled hot chocolate.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies a specific physical movement (reaching for a towel) and correctly attributes it to motor neurons.

sample_responses.response: The creature's motor neurons allow him to run through the village.
sample_responses.commentary: Applies motor neurons to a specific movement in the movie plot (running) — a CB-listed acceptable example.

sample_responses.response: Ruth reflexively jumps when Lynn startles her.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies a specific reflexive movement (jumping at being startled) — a CB-listed acceptable example of motor neuron function.

sample_responses.response: Ruth's motor neurons allow Lynn to flip on the lights and allow Ruth to watch the movie, taste the hot chocolate, and feel burned when she spills the hot chocolate on herself.
sample_responses.commentary: Credits motor neurons with multiple unrelated functions (watching, tasting, feeling, someone else's action) — unacceptable multi-function response.

parts.prompt: Explain how Somatosensory cortex relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate the contribution of the somatosensory cortex to Ruth's sense of touch — specifically temperature, body position, pressure, texture, or pain — in the context of the scenario. Responses that attribute other senses (taste, smell) or emotions ('feeling scared') to the somatosensory cortex do not earn the point.

required_elements: The somatosensory cortex processes tactile sensations (heat, pain, pressure, body position) from the scenario.
alternatives.correct_example: Ruth's somatosensory cortex allows her to feel the pain and heat after she spills hot chocolate on herself.

wrong_examples: Ruth's somatosensory cortex allows her to taste her delicious hot chocolate.
Ruth's somatosensory cortex allows her to feel scared when she is startled.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The somatosensory cortex (in the parietal lobe) processes body sensations: touch, temperature, pain, pressure, and body position. The response must connect this to a tactile sensation Ruth experiences — most obviously feeling the heat/pain from the spilled hot chocolate. Responses that credit the somatosensory cortex for taste or smell (different sensory modalities) or for emotional feelings rather than physical touch sensations do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Ruth's somatosensory cortex allows her to feel the pain and heat when she spills hot chocolate on herself.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies a tactile sensation (pain and temperature from the hot liquid) processed by the somatosensory cortex.

sample_responses.response: Ruth's somatosensory cortex allows her to sense the weight and texture of the hot chocolate mug in her hand.
sample_responses.commentary: Pressure and texture are additional touch sensations processed by the somatosensory cortex — this is a valid application.

sample_responses.response: Ruth's somatosensory cortex allows her to taste her delicious hot chocolate.
sample_responses.commentary: Taste is processed by the gustatory cortex, not the somatosensory cortex. The somatosensory cortex handles touch, temperature, pain, and body position.

sample_responses.response: Ruth's somatosensory cortex allows her to feel scared when Lynn startles her.
sample_responses.commentary: Fear is an emotional response involving the amygdala, not the somatosensory cortex. The somatosensory cortex processes physical touch sensations, not emotions.

parts.prompt: As they are cleaning up, Ruth tells Lynn the plot of the movie she was watching. While the creature was large and scary-looking, he was actually quite gentle and friendly. The villagers discussed the situation and concluded that the creature was going to hurt them, so they grabbed their torches and pitchforks and chased him out of town. As the creature tried to escape, he tripped over a rock and knocked down a villager. Explain how Out-group homogeneity bias relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that the villagers perceive creatures as all sharing the same (negative) characteristics — that all creatures are the same (dangerous, evil, etc.). The villagers are the in-group; the creatures are the out-group. Responses that treat villagers and the creature as part of the same group do not earn the point.

required_elements: The villagers assume all creatures share the same characteristics (e.g., all creatures are mean/evil/dangerous).
alternatives.correct_example: The villagers think that all creatures are mean and evil, so they assume this friendly creature must be dangerous too.

wrong_examples: The creatures and the villagers live in an area that has a lot of bias towards others.
The villager and the creature are in the same group, so they have homogeneity.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Out-group homogeneity bias is the tendency for people to perceive members of an out-group as more similar to each other than members of the in-group ('they all look/act the same'). The response must apply this to the villagers (in-group) perceiving the creature as belonging to an out-group (creatures) that all share the same dangerous/evil characteristics. Responses that treat villagers and the creature as the same group, or that describe general bias without the homogeneity component, do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: The villagers exhibit out-group homogeneity bias by assuming that all creatures are dangerous and evil — they see the creature as a member of a threatening out-group and assume he shares the same characteristics as all other creatures.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies villagers as the in-group, creatures as the out-group, and the assumption that all members of the out-group share the same (dangerous) traits.

sample_responses.response: The creatures and the villagers live in an area that has a lot of bias towards others.
sample_responses.commentary: Vague — treats villagers and creature as part of the same community without identifying in-group/out-group dynamics or the homogeneity assumption.

parts.prompt: As they are cleaning up, Ruth tells Lynn the plot of the movie she was watching. While the creature was large and scary-looking, he was actually quite gentle and friendly. The villagers discussed the situation and concluded that the creature was going to hurt them, so they grabbed their torches and pitchforks and chased him out of town. As the creature tried to escape, he tripped over a rock and knocked down a villager. Explain how Fundamental attribution error relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must contrast dispositional and situational attributions. The villagers attribute the creature's behavior (knocking over a villager) to a dispositional cause (he is evil/dangerous by nature) rather than the situational cause (he was being chased and tripped). Both the dispositional attribution AND the contrast with the situational cause must be present.

required_elements: The villagers attribute the creature's knocking over a villager to his evil nature (dispositional), ignoring that he was being chased and tripped (situational).
alternatives.correct_example: The villagers assume the creature knocked the villager over because he is evil rather than because he was being chased and tripped — this is the fundamental attribution error.

wrong_examples: The villagers think the creature is mean.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The fundamental attribution error is the tendency to overestimate the role of dispositional factors and underestimate situational factors when explaining others' behavior. The response must CONTRAST the dispositional attribution (the creature is evil/dangerous) with the situational explanation (he was being chased and tripped) — both elements must be present. Simply saying 'the villagers think the creature is mean' identifies a negative disposition but does not contrast it with the situational explanation.

sample_responses.response: The villagers commit the fundamental attribution error by assuming the creature knocked the villager over because he is inherently evil, rather than recognizing the situational cause — he was being chased by an angry mob and tripped.
sample_responses.commentary: Contrasts the dispositional attribution (inherently evil) with the situational explanation (being chased, tripped), demonstrating the fundamental attribution error.

sample_responses.response: The villagers think the creature is mean.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies a dispositional attribution but does not contrast it with the situational cause — the contrast is required to demonstrate the fundamental attribution error.

parts.prompt: As they are cleaning up, Ruth tells Lynn the plot of the movie she was watching. While the creature was large and scary-looking, he was actually quite gentle and friendly. The villagers discussed the situation and concluded that the creature was going to hurt them, so they grabbed their torches and pitchforks and chased him out of town. As the creature tried to escape, he tripped over a rock and knocked down a villager. Explain how Group polarization relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Two elements required: (1) villagers individually already feel threatened/scared of the creature before discussing it, AND (2) those feelings become more extreme after they meet to discuss what to do. Both elements must be present for credit.

required_elements: Villagers individually were already scared/threatened by the creature.
After group discussion, their fear/hostility became even more extreme.
alternatives.correct_example: Before the meeting, the villagers thought that the creature was terrible, and after the meeting they thought that the creature was even more terrible.

wrong_examples: Some villagers actually thought the creature was probably okay, and they should leave him alone, but they didn't say anything because they wanted to maintain group harmony.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Group polarization is the tendency for group discussion to strengthen the dominant pre-existing attitude of group members. The response must indicate BOTH (1) that the villagers individually are already threatened or scared of the creature, AND (2) that these feelings become more extreme when they meet to discuss what to do. A response that describes groupthink-style suppression of dissent (rather than an intensification of the dominant attitude) does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Before the meeting, the villagers thought that the creature was terrible, and after the meeting they thought that the creature was even more terrible.
sample_responses.commentary: Both required elements present: (1) pre-existing individual belief that the creature is terrible, (2) that belief became more extreme after group discussion — a verbatim CB acceptable example.

sample_responses.response: Some villagers actually thought the creature was probably okay, and they should leave him alone, but they didn't say anything because they wanted to maintain group harmony.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes groupthink (suppression of dissent to preserve harmony), not group polarization (intensification of a pre-existing dominant attitude through discussion).

parts.prompt: Explain why Lynn's assumption is likely incorrect.

scoring_points.description: The response must explain why Lynn's assumption that Ruth was sleepwalking during a dream (REM sleep) is incorrect. Two acceptable paths: (1) during REM sleep the body is paralyzed/immobile, making sleepwalking impossible, OR (2) sleepwalking occurs during NREM deep sleep (Stage 3/4), not during REM when dreaming occurs.

required_elements: During REM sleep the body is paralyzed, so Ruth could not sleepwalk.
alternatives.correct_example: It is highly unlikely that Ruth was sleepwalking while in REM sleep because during REM sleep the muscles of the body are paralyzed, so she wouldn't be able to walk around.

required_elements: Sleepwalking occurs during NREM (deep) sleep, not during REM (dreaming) sleep.
alternatives.correct_example: Sleepwalking occurs during deep NREM sleep, not during the REM stage when dreaming takes place — so Ruth could not have been dreaming and sleepwalking at the same time.

wrong_examples: Lynn's assumption is incorrect because if Ruth was acting out a dream of being chased, she would be running around, not walking slowly.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Lynn assumes Ruth was sleepwalking while acting out a dream. The response must indicate that the assumption is likely incorrect because either (1) during REM sleep the body is paralyzed / immobile, OR (2) sleepwalking and dreaming typically occur in different stages of sleep. Responses that critique the pace or style of Ruth's movements, rather than identifying the physiological incompatibility between REM sleep and motor activity, do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: It is highly unlikely that Ruth was in REM sleep because during REM sleep the muscles of the body are paralyzed, so she wouldn't be able to walk around.
sample_responses.commentary: Verbatim CB acceptable example — identifies REM-sleep muscle paralysis as the reason sleepwalking during a dream is implausible.

sample_responses.response: Sleepwalking typically occurs during NREM deep sleep, not during the REM stage when dreaming takes place, so Ruth could not have been acting out a dream while sleepwalking.
sample_responses.commentary: Applies the alternate CB rationale — sleepwalking and dreaming occur in different sleep stages.

sample_responses.response: Lynn's assumption is incorrect because if Ruth was acting out a dream of being chased, she would be running around, not walking slowly.
sample_responses.commentary: Critiques Ruth's movement style rather than identifying the physiological reason (REM atonia or different sleep stages) the assumption is implausible.
